decisions.title: Expand café to downtown business district
decisions.statement: Open a second café location in the downtown business district this fall.
decisions.context: Our single shop is at capacity on weekday mornings; a downtown storefront just opened up with three months of free rent; the owner wants to grow before a competitor takes the spot.

assumptions.text: Downtown foot traffic will support sales close to what our current location does.
assumptions.signpost: Weekday foot-traffic counts, or early sales at the new site, coming in well below the current shop

assumptions.text: The business has adequate staffing, supply chain capacity, and operational systems to manage two locations simultaneously without degrading service quality.
assumptions.signpost: Customer complaints about wait times or order errors increase at the original location; staff turnover accelerates; supplier delivery delays or stockouts occur; order-to-service time exceeds historical averages at either location.

assumptions.text: The new location will not pull a large share of sales away from our existing shop.
assumptions.signpost: Existing-store sales dropping once the downtown location opens.

assumptions.text: The build-out will be finished before the three months of free rent run out.
assumptions.signpost: The contractor timeline slipping past the free-rent window.

evidence.text: A pedestrian-count study of the downtown block shows about 40 percent more weekday foot traffic than the street our current café is on.
Our last two barista job postings each took about six weeks to fill, and one of those new hires left within a month
A quick survey of our regulars found that roughly a third of them work downtown and say they would visit the new location instead of the current one
The contractor confirmed the build-out is on track to finish about two weeks before the free-rent period ends.

decisions.title: Switch the team to a new chat app
decisions.statement: Move our whole team off our current chat app onto a new one this quarter.
decisions.context: Our current chat tool keeps going down and the new one is cheaper, and leadership wants the switch done before the current contract renews.

assumptions.text: The team will actually adopt the new app instead of drifting back to the old tool or email.
assumptions.signpost: Message volume on the new app staying low, or people still working in the old tool weeks after the switch.

assumptions.text: The new app connects to the other tools we rely on, like our calendar, file storage, and ticketing.
assumptions.signpost: A key integration missing or breaking after the switch.

assumptions.text: The new app will not end up costing more than the tool we are replacing once everyone is added.
assumptions.signpost: Per-user pricing tiers pushing the bill above what we pay today.

assumptions.text: We can move everyone over before the current contract renews.
assumptions.signpost: The rollout slipping past the renewal date.

evidence.text: A pilot with the marketing team saw about 90 percent of them using the new app every day within two weeks.
IT found the new app does not yet connect to our ticketing system, so linking a chat to a ticket has to be done by hand.
Finance pointed out that above 200 users the new app jumps to a higher price tier, which would wipe out the savings.
The rollout plan is on track to finish about a month before the current contract renews.

decisions.title: Outsource fulfillment to a 3PL by Q2
decisions.statement: Move e-commerce fulfillment from our in-house warehouse to a third-party logistics provider (3PL) by Q2.
decisions.context: Order volume up 60 percent year over year; warehouse lease renewal due in 8 months; 3PL quotes suggest 18 percent cost savings at current volume.

assumptions.text: The 3PL can match our in-house shipping speed and accuracy closely enough that customers don't notice.
assumptions.signpost: Pilot same-day-ship rate or mispick rate diverging from in-house baselines; shipping complaints rising.

assumptions.text: The quoted 18 percent cost savings will hold at our actual volumes and SKU count.
assumptions.signpost: Pricing-tier triggers in the contract, such as SKU count or volume thresholds, coming into play.

assumptions.text: The 3PL will remain stable and able to onboard us fully before our warehouse lease decision.
assumptions.signpost: Ownership changes, onboarding freezes, or client-reported service disruptions at the 3PL.

assumptions.text: Our product data feed is clean enough to drive a third party's pick-and-pack operation.
assumptions.signpost: Fulfillment errors traced to catalog or data-feed issues rather than 3PL execution.

evidence.text: 3PL pilot, week 3: 94 percent of orders shipped same-day vs our 97 percent in-house. Two mispicks on bundled SKUs, both traced to our product data feed.
Post-purchase survey trend: 'fast shipping' mentions in 5-star reviews dropped from 31 percent to 24 percent during the pilot region rollout.
The 3PL announced acquisition by a larger logistics group; two of their mid-size clients publicly flagged onboarding freezes during the transition.
Ops review: 3PL pricing assumes our current SKU count. Planned Q3 product line expansion adds 40 SKUs, which triggers their next pricing tier.
